channel.title: learning on Patrick Armengol
channel.description: Recent content in learning on Patrick Armengol

item.title: Backend Learning Plan
item.description: Skills Languages Python Golang Python Frameworks Django Flask FastAPI Golang Frameworks Gin Beego Databases MySQL SQLite PostgreSQL MongoDB ORMs APIs REST GraphQL Containers Docker kubernetes Authentication and Authorization JSON web tokens cookies and sessions OAuth (Google, GitHub) auth libraries (passport, grant) password hashing (bcrypt) protecting endpoints and routes Hosting Platforms DigitalOcean Linode heroku Books ??? Project Ideas space news dashboard kanji of the day srs flashcards like kanji.koohii endangered species tracker site url shortener minimal markdown recipe site cli downloader with progress bars Blog Post Ideas exploring concurrency in python API framework comparison

item.title: Frontend Learning Plan
item.description: Skills Javascript basics dom async fetch + http array methods TypeScript HTML HTML5 basic and semantic tags page layout CSS fundamentals (colors, fonts, borders, margins) alignment (flexbox, css grid) media queries responsive design animations and transitions Bootstrap Tailwind CSS daisyUI Tools package management Emmet extension Live Server extension Prettier extension ESLint extension Platforms Netlify Frontend Frameworks Vue.js Vue CLI Vue Router Composition API Vuex Server Side Rendering Nuxt.js Creative Coding p5.

item.title: Data Science Learning Plan
item.description: Skills Python see Python Learning Plan Data Analysis numpy pandas scipy Data Visualization matplotlib seaborn panel Scraping beautifulsoup scrapy Excel basic understanding of functions Database MySQL SQLite PostgreSQL MongoDB Statistics ??? Machine Learning ??? Books Python for Data Analysis Python Data Science Handbook Fundamentals of Data Visualization Storytelling with Data Practical Statistics for Data Scientists Naked Statistics Machine Learning Simplified The Hundred-Page Machine Learning Book Hands-On Machine Learning with Scikit-Learn, Keras, and TensorFlow Designing Machine Learning Systems Project Ideas web scraping to create a dataset air quality data analysis nobel prize winner data analysis arxiv submission data analysis TM COTD data analysis hydrogen fuel data analysis data vis dashboard with panel

item.title: Job Hunt
item.description: Background It has now been over a year since I quit my last job. This year-long “sabbatical” has been an incredible experience and I’m glad that I ended up taking it. I absolutely love travel. I love exploring new places, seeing cool stuff, meeting interesting people, trying different cuisines. I love the alone time in the woods. I love exercising every day. But I can see that I’m growing weary of it.

item.title: Review: Problem Solving with Algorithms and Data Structures using Python
item.description: This is my short review of “Problem Solving with Algorithms and Data Structures using Python” by Brad Miller and David Ranum. About This book is published online for free as an interactive textbook at https://runestone.academy/ns/books/published/pythonds3/index.html I found this book through a recommendation on reddit when I was searching for a somewhat comprehensive DSA book with Python code. Things I liked fairly comprehensive code your own data structures tons of suggested exercises at the end of each chapter real world relevance discussed Things I disliked only a few mentions of existing libraries that implement/optimize certain algorithms or data structures coding style was a bit strange; some implementations were a bit convoluted and focused too much on the example problem task many errata, especially in the figures and code some explanations were lacking Conclusion This book ended up being very close to what I was looking for.

item.title: Review: Dive Into Algorithms
item.description: This is my short review of “Dive Into Algorithms” by Bradford Tuckfield. About This book is marketed as an intro to algorithms for beginners. Things I liked real world relevance introduction to more advanced/specialized concepts math Things I disliked the author’s writing style the author’s coding style the author’s deviation from PEP 8 the exclusion of data structures non-comprehensivity (is that a word?) of which algorithms were introduced weirdly complex examples for beginners nested list comprehensions Conclusion I was looking for a book that would help me refresh my knowledge of data structures and algorithms.

item.title: Review: Beyond the Basic Stuff with Python
item.description: This is my short review of “Beyond the Basic Stuff with Python” by Al Sweigart. About This book is a followup to “Automate the Basic Stuff with Python” and is meant to bridge the gap from beginner to intermediate. I started reading it just after the first book as I thought it was appropriate and would help give me a better understanding of the language, its features, and best practices. My idea was that I should round out my technical knowledge before jumping in to the more project based books.

item.title: Review: Automate the Boring Stuff with Python
item.description: This is my short review of “Automate the Boring Stuff with Python” by Al Sweigart. About This book is aimed at absolute beginners of python and/or programming. I read it as the first book in my python re-learning journey. Prior to reading, I already knew quite a bit of python, but I chose to read it page by page, following all the examples, and doing most of the projects, because I suck at python and want to suck a little bit less.

item.title: Japanese Learning Plan
item.description: Why I like Japan. I like Japanese food, comedy, anime, music, architecture, games, and tea. Am I weird for liking Japan so much? Some people would say otherwise, but I don’t think so; it’s just one of the places/cultures on this planet that interest me. I’ve been to Japan three times; the first for a weekend, then for 2 weeks, then for 2 months. Though I thoroughly enjoyed my time there, I wish I had visited more of the non-touristy bits.

item.title: Python Learning Plan
item.description: Why Why am I learning python? Well… technically I’m re-learning it. You don’t get a degree in computer engineering without knowing how to program, and I’d say I’m fairly proficient competent at python. Heck, I used it every day in my previous job, which involved: creating threat hunting playbooks in Jupyter notebooks, writing quick automation scripts, building internal tools, wrangling telemetry, interacting with APIs, and disecting malware programatically. So why exactly am I brushing up on python?
